title: Truite aux amandes
subtitle: Pan-fried trout with browned butter and toasted almonds
excerpt: Trout fillets dusted in seasoned flour and fried in butter until golden, then finished with flaked almonds toasted in foaming brown butter and a squeeze of lemon. Eight minutes of cooking, one pan, nothing wasted.
sourceNotes: Truite aux amandes is a staple of the French bistro repertoire, documented from at least the early twentieth century. It is a variant of à la meunière technique (the fish dusted in flour, fried in butter, finished with lemon) but with the addition of flaked almonds toasted in the same butter. It appears in Escoffier's 'Le Guide Culinaire' as part of the trout section.

content.text: The dish has two components: the fish and the beurre noisette. The fish cooks in the first round of butter; you pour that off, wipe the pan, and cook the almonds in fresh butter that you take to the nut-brown stage — beurre noisette. The nutty, slightly caramelised smell is the signal. Pour it over the fish immediately; brown butter goes from perfect to burned in under a minute.

Trout fillets are thin and cook fast. The skin goes down first into hot butter and flour; three minutes gives you a crisp, golden skin and the flesh is already nearly cooked through. One minute on the flesh side finishes it. If the fillet is thick (from a large rainbow trout or sea trout), add 30 seconds each side.

Pat the {{trout-fillet}} fillets dry and season well on both sides. Dust lightly in {{plain-flour}}, shaking off the excess. Melt 40 g of the {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a wide frying pan over medium-high heat until foaming. Lay the fillets skin-side down and press gently with a spatula for the first 30 seconds to stop them curling. Cook for 3 minutes until the skin is golden, then turn and cook flesh-side down for 1 minute. Transfer to warm plates.
Make the brown butter and almonds

Wipe the pan clean with kitchen paper. Add the remaining 40 g of butter and the {{almonds-flaked}} to the pan over medium heat. Cook, stirring often, for 2 to 3 minutes until the butter is golden-brown and smells nutty, and the almonds are toasted. The moment the butter colours, add a squeeze of the {{lemon}} (it will spit) then pour over the fillets immediately. Scatter {{parsley-flat}} over and serve at once.

items.symptom: Skin did not crisp and stuck to the pan
items.cause: Pan not hot enough, or fish not dry before flouring
items.fix: Pat fillets very dry; get the butter hot before adding the fish; press down in the first 30 seconds to ensure full skin contact

items.symptom: Brown butter burned and tastes bitter
items.cause: Pan too hot or left unattended
items.fix: Watch constantly once the butter starts colouring; the almonds help indicate colour but the butter leads — when it goes hazelnut-brown, it's ready

items.symptom: Flesh has broken up when turned
items.cause: Turned before the skin side was fully set
items.fix: Do not rush the first 3 minutes on the skin side; the flesh will release cleanly from the pan when it's ready to turn

items.symptom: Flour coating is gummy rather than crisp
items.cause: Too much flour, or flour was left to sit and absorb moisture
items.fix: Dust lightly and cook immediately; a thick flour coat goes paste-like before the fish cooks through

content.text: Truite aux amandes occupies the same bistro ledger entry as sole meunière: a fine fish, cooked simply in butter, served with the same butter browned and poured back over. The almond addition is distinctly French — almonds appear in classical French cooking as a textural garnish in butter sauces for delicate fish and poultry, a tradition that traces back to medieval haute cuisine and persisted through the bistro era. The dish is weeknight-fast and dinner-party presentable without being the same thing in different contexts.
